episode_text:
day=14; meal=chicken nuggets, broccoli, and ketchup; setting=Personal; time_of_day=morning;
preferences: microwave_time=2 min; occlusion_relevance=do not consider occlusion; robot_speed=medium; skewering_axis=perpendicular to major axis; web_interface_confirmation=no; transfer_mode=outside mouth transfer; outside_mouth_distance=medium; convey_robot_ready_for_initiating_transfer=speech + LED; detect_user_ready_for_initiating_transfer_feeding=open mouth; detect_user_ready_for_initiating_transfer_drinking=button; detect_user_ready_for_initiating_transfer_wiping=button; convey_robot_ready_for_completing_transfer=speech + LED; detect_user_completed_transfer_feeding=perception; detect_user_completed_transfer_drinking=button; detect_user_completed_transfer_wiping=button; retract_between_bites=no; bite_dipping_preference=less; wait_before_autocontinue_seconds=100 sec

microwave_time.user_tendencies: IF food is a cold-served item such as fruit or dessert AND any dining context AND any affective state THEN no microwave BECAUSE the user repeatedly selected no microwave for cold meals, including social and right-TV fruit/dessert cases, preserving intended serving temperature. IF dining context is a quiet personal setting AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a typical warm entree or warm snack meal THEN 2 min BECAUSE this remains the best-supported personal default and was again selected in the new personal warm meal. IF dining context is social with a person on the right AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a typical warm entree THEN 2 min BECAUSE the user selected standard reheating in that social context. IF dining context is social with a person on the left AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a cold fruit/dessert meal THEN no microwave BECAUSE the left-social cold meal matches the broader cold-meal pattern rather than indicating a social-specific heating change. IF dining context involves watching TV in front AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a typical warm entree or warm snack meal THEN heating preference is mixed, with both 1 min and 3 min observed, so 2 min is the most stable front-TV summary BECAUSE the user does not consistently want lighter or heavier reheating in that context. IF dining context involves watching TV on the right AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a cold fruit/dessert meal THEN no microwave BECAUSE the right-TV strawberries meal follows the stable cold-food rule. IF dining context involves watching TV on the right AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a typical warm entree THEN 2 min BECAUSE the user selected standard reheating for a warm TV meal on the right. IF dining context involves watching TV on the left AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a typical warm entree THEN 2 min BECAUSE the user also selected standard reheating in that media context. IF dining context involves working on a laptop/tablet on the left AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a typical warm entree THEN 1 min BECAUSE the user selected lighter reheating for one left-work warm meal. IF dining context involves working on a laptop/tablet on the right AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a typical warm entree THEN 3 min BECAUSE the user selected the hottest reheating level in that right-work meal, showing work-context temperature preference can vary by setup.

occlusion_relevance.user_tendencies: IF dining context includes a person on the right OR a TV/device on the right AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a typical warm entree or other non-cold meal THEN minimize right occlusion BECAUSE the user repeatedly chose to avoid blocking salient right-side social or visual targets in those contexts. IF dining context involves watching TV on the right AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a cold fruit/dessert meal THEN do not consider occlusion BECAUSE the right-TV strawberries meal shows the user does not always prioritize right-side visibility for lighter cold meals. IF dining context is social with a person on the left AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food has no special constraint THEN minimize left occlusion BECAUSE the user selected left-side occlusion minimization when a social partner was on the left. IF dining context involves watching TV in front AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food has no special constraint THEN minimize front occlusion BECAUSE the user repeatedly selected front-occlusion minimization when the salient target was directly ahead. IF dining context involves watching TV on the left AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food has no special constraint THEN do not consider occlusion BECAUSE the user did not request left-side occlusion minimization in that media context. IF dining context involves working on a laptop/tablet on the left AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a typical warm entree or a cold fruit meal THEN do not consider occlusion BECAUSE the user repeatedly declined occlusion-based adjustment in left-side work contexts. IF dining context is a quiet personal setting AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food has no special constraint THEN do not consider occlusion BECAUSE both prior and new personal meals show no need for occlusion-based adjustment without a salient social or media target.

robot_speed.user_tendencies: IF dining context is social with a person on the right AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a typical warm entree THEN medium BECAUSE the user selected medium speed in that social setting, reinforcing medium as a comfortable socially appropriate pace. IF dining context is social with a person on the left AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a cold fruit/dessert meal THEN medium BECAUSE the left-social meal preserved the same moderate pace, suggesting social meals favor comfort over maximum speed. IF dining context involves watching TV in front AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a light cold item or a warm snack/entree THEN speed preference is mixed between fast and medium, with newer front-TV evidence favoring medium, so medium is the more conservative front-TV summary BECAUSE the user does not always prioritize maximum speed when watching TV ahead. IF dining context involves watching TV on the right AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a typical warm entree or a cold fruit/dessert meal THEN medium BECAUSE both observed right-TV meals used medium speed, indicating a stable moderate pace in that context. IF dining context involves watching TV on the left AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a typical warm entree THEN medium BECAUSE the user selected medium speed in that context. IF dining context involves working on a laptop/tablet on the left AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a typical warm entree THEN fast BECAUSE the user selected fast speed while working, indicating that some task-focused meals prioritize efficiency. IF dining context involves working on a laptop/tablet on the left AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a cold fruit meal THEN medium BECAUSE the user selected medium speed for fruit while working, suggesting the work-context preference for speed is moderated by meal type. IF dining context involves working on a laptop/tablet on the right AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a typical warm entree THEN medium BECAUSE the user selected medium speed in that right-side work context. IF dining context is a quiet personal setting AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food has no special constraint THEN medium BECAUSE this remains the best-supported comfortable default and was again selected in the new personal meal.

transfer_mode.user_tendencies: IF dining context is social with a person on the right AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food has no special constraint THEN outside mouth transfer BECAUSE the user selected the less intrusive transfer style in that social setting. IF dining context is social with a person on the left AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a cold fruit/dessert meal THEN inside mouth transfer BECAUSE the left-social meal shows that in some social contexts the user prefers lower-effort direct delivery rather than the more conservative outside-mouth style. IF dining context involves watching TV in front AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food has no special constraint THEN inside mouth transfer BECAUSE the user repeatedly selected direct delivery in front-TV contexts, indicating a stable preference to reduce the need to lean forward while attending to media ahead. IF dining context involves watching TV on the right AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a cold fruit/dessert meal THEN inside mouth transfer BECAUSE the right-TV strawberries meal aligns with a lower-effort media-focused strategy. IF dining context involves watching TV on the right AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a typical warm entree THEN outside mouth transfer BECAUSE the user selected the less intrusive, more user-controlled style in that context. IF dining context involves watching TV on the left AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food has no special constraint THEN inside mouth transfer BECAUSE the user selected inside-mouth transfer in that media context. IF dining context involves working on a laptop/tablet on the left AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a typical warm entree or a cold fruit meal THEN inside mouth transfer BECAUSE the user repeatedly selected direct delivery while working, showing that active device engagement favors reduced user movement. IF dining context involves working on a laptop/tablet on the right AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a typical warm entree THEN inside mouth transfer BECAUSE the user also selected direct delivery in that right-side work context. IF dining context is a quiet personal setting AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food has no special constraint THEN outside mouth transfer BECAUSE this remains the conservative personal default, is physically feasible for the user, and was again selected in the new personal meal.

outside_mouth_distance.user_tendencies: IF transfer mode is outside mouth transfer AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food has no special constraint THEN medium BECAUSE the user selected a moderate stopping distance that balances comfort and reachability, including in social, right-TV, and the new personal outside-mouth meal. IF transfer mode is inside mouth transfer AND any dining context AND any affective state AND any food condition THEN not applicable BECAUSE this parameter only applies to outside mouth transfer.

convey_robot_ready_for_initiating_transf.user_tendencies: IF dining context is social with a person on the right AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food has no special constraint THEN LED BECAUSE the user selected a quiet visual cue in that social setting. IF dining context is social with a person on the left AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a cold fruit/dessert meal THEN speech + LED BECAUSE the left-social meal shows the user may want a more explicit multimodal cue in some social settings, likely to ensure clear coordination while attention is shared with a partner. IF dining context involves watching TV in front AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food has no special constraint THEN front-TV cue preference is mixed between no cue and LED, with repeated evidence favoring reduced interruption but later evidence showing acceptance of a quiet visual cue, so LED is the best-supported conservative summary BECAUSE it preserves low disruption without fully suppressing readiness information. IF dining context involves watching TV on the right AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a cold fruit/dessert meal THEN LED BECAUSE the right-TV strawberries meal shows the user can prefer a quieter visual-only cue when attention is on a right-side screen during a light meal. IF dining context involves watching TV on the right AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a typical warm entree THEN speech + LED BECAUSE the user selected the most explicit multimodal cue in that right-TV warm meal, suggesting stronger notification can be preferred when attention is directed to a right-side screen. IF dining context involves watching TV on the left AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food has no special constraint THEN LED BECAUSE the user selected LED rather than suppressing cues. IF dining context involves working on a laptop/tablet on the left AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a typical warm entree THEN no cue BECAUSE the user selected no cue while working on a warm entree, indicating a preference to suppress readiness notifications during some task-focused meals. IF dining context involves working on a laptop/tablet on the left AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a cold fruit meal THEN speech BECAUSE the user selected speech in the same work context for fruit, showing that cue preference can shift with meal type. IF dining context involves working on a laptop/tablet on the right AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a typical warm entree THEN speech + LED BECAUSE the user selected multimodal cueing in that right-side work context. IF dining context is a quiet personal setting AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a typical warm entree with sauce or dip available THEN speech + LED BECAUSE the new personal sauced meal used a more explicit multimodal cue, suggesting the user may sometimes want stronger coordination in personal meals with more complex bite handling. IF dining context is a quiet personal setting AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food has no special constraint THEN LED BECAUSE this remains the conservative low-burden default outside observed task-focused suppression or stronger multimodal cueing in specific meals.

detect_user_ready_for_initiating_transfe.user_tendencies: IF dining context is social with a person on the right AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food has no special constraint THEN button BECAUSE the user repeatedly chose button-based initiation in social meals and can comfortably press buttons, consistent with avoiding ambiguity from open-mouth signals during conversation. IF dining context is social with a person on the left AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a cold fruit/dessert meal THEN open mouth BECAUSE the left-social meal shows a lower-effort social strategy can be preferred for a light meal, so social context does not always force explicit manual control. IF dining context involves watching TV in front AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food has no special constraint THEN front-TV preference is mixed between autocontinue and button, with newer evidence showing explicit control, so button is the more cautious front-TV summary BECAUSE the user may want deliberate timing even while attending to media ahead. IF dining context involves watching TV on the right AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a cold fruit/dessert meal THEN button BECAUSE the right-TV strawberries meal shows explicit manual initiation can be preferred in that media context for a light meal. IF dining context involves watching TV on the right AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a typical warm entree THEN open mouth BECAUSE the user preferred low-effort perceptual initiation for feeding in that context rather than manual confirmation. IF dining context involves watching TV on the left AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food has no special constraint THEN button BECAUSE the user selected manual readiness signaling in that side-TV context. IF dining context involves working on a laptop/tablet on the left AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a typical warm entree THEN autocontinue BECAUSE the user selected automatic initiation while working, suggesting task-focused contexts can shift preference from explicit control toward reduced interaction burden. IF dining context involves working on a laptop/tablet on the left AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a cold fruit meal THEN open mouth BECAUSE the user selected open-mouth initiation for feeding in that work-and-fruit context, showing that for feeding the user may accept low-effort perceptual initiation rather than button press when the meal is light. IF dining context involves working on a laptop/tablet on the right AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a typical warm entree THEN open mouth BECAUSE the user selected open-mouth initiation in that right-side work context. IF dining context is a quiet personal setting AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a warm meal with sauce or dip available THEN open mouth BECAUSE the new personal sauced meal used open-mouth initiation, indicating that in non-social settings the user may accept lower-effort perceptual feeding initiation for such meals.

IF dining context is social with a person on the right AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food has no special constraint THEN button BECAUSE the user repeatedly preferred manual readiness signaling for drinking in social settings and can comfortably press buttons. IF dining context is social with a person on the left AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a cold fruit/dessert meal THEN open mouth BECAUSE the left-social meal shows the user may trade explicit control for lower effort in some social meals, especially lighter ones. IF dining context involves watching TV in front AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food has no special constraint THEN button BECAUSE later front-TV evidence selected explicit manual readiness, making button the more stable summary for drinking. IF dining context involves watching TV on the right AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a typical warm entree or a cold fruit/dessert meal THEN button BECAUSE both observed right-TV meals used explicit manual readiness for drinking, indicating a stable preference for deliberate sip timing in that context. IF dining context involves watching TV on the left AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food has no special constraint THEN button BECAUSE the user again selected manual readiness signaling, reinforcing a side-TV preference for explicit control. IF dining context involves working on a laptop/tablet on the left AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food has no special constraint THEN evidence is mixed between autocontinue and button, with the specific fruit meal favoring button, so button is the more conservative summary for drinking in left-work contexts BECAUSE the user often prefers deliberate sip timing. IF dining context involves working on a laptop/tablet on the left AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a cold fruit meal THEN button BECAUSE the user selected explicit manual readiness for drinking in that specific work-and-fruit context, indicating more deliberate control for sip timing than for feeding. IF dining context involves working on a laptop/tablet on the right AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a typical warm entree THEN open mouth BECAUSE the user selected open-mouth initiation for drinking in that right-side work context. IF dining context is a quiet personal setting AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food has no special constraint THEN button BECAUSE the new personal meal also used manual readiness for drinking, reinforcing button as the stable personal default.

IF dining context is social with a person on the right AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food has no special constraint THEN button BECAUSE the user repeatedly preferred manual readiness signaling for wiping in social settings and can comfortably press buttons. IF dining context is social with a person on the left AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a cold fruit/dessert meal THEN open mouth BECAUSE the left-social meal shows the user may prefer lower-effort perceptual initiation for wiping in some social meals. IF dining context involves watching TV in front AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food has no special constraint THEN button BECAUSE later front-TV evidence selected explicit manual readiness, making button the more stable summary for wiping. IF dining context involves watching TV on the right AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a typical warm entree or a cold fruit/dessert meal THEN button BECAUSE both observed right-TV meals used manual readiness signaling for wiping, indicating a stable preference for explicit control in that context. IF dining context involves watching TV on the left AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food has no special constraint THEN button BECAUSE the user again selected manual readiness signaling, reinforcing a side-TV preference for explicit control. IF dining context involves working on a laptop/tablet on the left AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a typical warm entree THEN autocontinue BECAUSE the user selected automatic initiation while working, extending the pattern that task-focused contexts can favor lower-effort interaction. IF dining context involves working on a laptop/tablet on the left AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a cold fruit meal THEN button BECAUSE the user selected explicit manual readiness for wiping in that specific work-and-fruit context, indicating more deliberate control for wiping timing than the earlier work default suggested. IF dining context involves working on a laptop/tablet on the right AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a typical warm entree THEN open mouth BECAUSE the user selected open-mouth initiation in that right-side work context. IF dining context is a quiet personal setting AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food has no special constraint THEN button BECAUSE the new personal meal also used manual readiness for wiping, reinforcing button as the stable personal default.

convey_robot_ready_for_completing_transf.user_tendencies: IF dining context is social with a person on the right AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food has no special constraint THEN LED BECAUSE the user selected a quiet visual completion cue in that social setting. IF dining context is social with a person on the left AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a cold fruit/dessert meal THEN speech + LED BECAUSE the left-social meal shows the user may want a more explicit multimodal completion cue in some social settings. IF dining context involves watching TV in front AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food has no special constraint THEN front-TV completion cue preference is mixed between no cue and LED, with later evidence showing acceptance of a quiet visual cue, so LED is the best-supported conservative summary BECAUSE it preserves low interruption while still marking transfer completion. IF dining context involves watching TV on the right AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a cold fruit/dessert meal THEN LED BECAUSE the right-TV strawberries meal shows the user can prefer a quieter visual-only completion cue during a light media-focused meal. IF dining context involves watching TV on the right AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a typical warm entree THEN speech + LED BECAUSE the user selected multimodal completion cueing in that right-TV warm meal, suggesting stronger notification can be preferred when attention is directed to a right-side screen. IF dining context involves watching TV on the left AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food has no special constraint THEN LED BECAUSE the user selected LED in that media context. IF dining context involves working on a laptop/tablet on the left AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a typical warm entree THEN no cue BECAUSE the user selected no cue while working, indicating a preference to suppress completion notifications during some attention-demanding meals. IF dining context involves working on a laptop/tablet on the left AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a cold fruit meal THEN speech BECAUSE the user selected speech in that work-and-fruit context, showing that completion cue preference can vary with meal type. IF dining context involves working on a laptop/tablet on the right AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a typical warm entree THEN speech + LED BECAUSE the user selected multimodal completion cueing in that right-side work context. IF dining context is a quiet personal setting AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a typical warm entree with sauce or dip available THEN speech + LED BECAUSE the new personal sauced meal used a more explicit multimodal completion cue, suggesting the user may sometimes want stronger coordination in personal meals with more complex bite handling. IF dining context is a quiet personal setting AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food has no special constraint THEN LED BECAUSE this remains the conservative low-burden default outside observed task-focused suppression or stronger multimodal cueing in specific meals.

detect_user_completed_transfer_drinking.user_tendencies: IF dining context is social with a person on the right AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food has no special constraint THEN button BECAUSE the user repeatedly chose button-based completion for drinking in social meals, favoring explicit control over automatic inference. IF dining context is social with a person on the left AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a cold fruit/dessert meal THEN autocontinue BECAUSE the left-social meal shows the user may prefer lower-effort automatic completion for drinking in some social meals. IF dining context involves watching TV in front AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food has no special constraint THEN evidence is mixed between autocontinue and button, with later front-TV evidence selecting button, so button is now the more stable front-TV summary BECAUSE the user may want explicit control over sip completion even when attention is directed to media ahead. IF dining context involves watching TV on the right AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a typical warm entree or a cold fruit/dessert meal THEN button BECAUSE both observed right-TV meals used button-based drinking completion, indicating a stable preference for explicit sip completion in that context. IF dining context involves watching TV on the left AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food has no special constraint THEN button BECAUSE the user again selected button-based completion, reinforcing a side-TV preference for explicit control. IF dining context involves working on a laptop/tablet on the left AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a typical warm entree THEN button BECAUSE the user kept explicit button-based completion while working, suggesting that even when initiation is automated the user prefers deliberate completion control for drinking. IF dining context involves working on a laptop/tablet on the left AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a cold fruit meal THEN button BECAUSE the user again selected button-based completion, strengthening the pattern that drinking completion remains explicitly user-controlled in left-side work contexts. IF dining context involves working on a laptop/tablet on the right AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a typical warm entree THEN autocontinue BECAUSE the user selected automatic completion in that right-side work context, showing that work can shift drinking completion toward lower-effort automation. IF dining context is a quiet personal setting AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food has no special constraint THEN button BECAUSE the new personal meal also used button-based drinking completion, reinforcing button as the stable personal default.

IF dining context is social with a person on the left AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a cold fruit/dessert meal THEN autocontinue BECAUSE the left-social meal shows the user may prefer lower-effort automatic completion for wiping in some social meals. IF dining context involves watching TV in front AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food has no special constraint THEN evidence is mixed between autocontinue and button, with later front-TV evidence selecting button, so button is now the more stable front-TV summary BECAUSE the user may want explicit control over wipe completion even when attention is directed to media ahead. IF dining context involves watching TV on the right AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a typical warm entree or a cold fruit/dessert meal THEN button BECAUSE both observed right-TV meals used button-based wiping completion, indicating a stable preference for explicit wipe completion in that context. IF dining context involves watching TV on the left AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food has no special constraint THEN button BECAUSE the user again selected button-based completion, reinforcing a side-TV preference for explicit control. IF dining context involves working on a laptop/tablet on the left AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a typical warm entree THEN button BECAUSE the user kept explicit button-based completion while working, suggesting that even when initiation is automated the user prefers deliberate completion control for wiping. IF dining context involves working on a laptop/tablet on the left AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a cold fruit meal THEN button BECAUSE the user again selected button-based completion, strengthening the pattern that wiping completion remains explicitly user-controlled in left-side work contexts. IF dining context involves working on a laptop/tablet on the right AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a typical warm entree THEN autocontinue BECAUSE the user selected automatic completion in that right-side work context, showing that work can shift wiping completion toward lower-effort automation. IF dining context is a quiet personal setting AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food has no special constraint THEN button BECAUSE the new personal meal also used button-based wiping completion, reinforcing button as the stable personal default.

retract_between_bites.user_tendencies: IF dining context includes a person on the right AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food has no special constraint THEN yes BECAUSE the user chose to reduce prolonged obstruction in that right-side social context, even at the cost of longer meal time. IF dining context is social with a person on the left AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a cold fruit/dessert meal THEN no BECAUSE the left-social meal shows the user does not always want extra retract motions in social settings, especially for a light meal. IF dining context includes a TV/device on the right AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a cold fruit/dessert meal THEN yes BECAUSE the right-TV strawberries meal shows the user may accept longer meal time to reduce prolonged obstruction during a light media-focused meal. IF dining context includes a TV/device on the right AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a typical warm entree THEN no BECAUSE the earlier right-TV warm meal did not use extra retract motions, indicating that right-side visual relevance alone does not always justify the time cost of retraction. IF dining context involves watching TV in front AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food has no special constraint THEN preference is mixed, with both no and yes observed, so front-TV meals show context sensitivity rather than a single stable rule BECAUSE the user sometimes accepts longer meals to reduce obstruction when the salient target is directly ahead. IF dining context involves watching TV on the left AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food has no special constraint THEN no BECAUSE the user also skipped retraction in that context. IF dining context involves working on a laptop/tablet on the left AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a typical warm entree THEN no BECAUSE the user skipped retraction while working on that meal, reinforcing a general preference to avoid extra time costs in many work contexts. IF dining context involves working on a laptop/tablet on the left AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a cold fruit meal THEN yes BECAUSE the user selected retraction in that specific work-and-fruit context, showing that when working the user may sometimes trade efficiency for reduced obstruction depending on the meal. IF dining context is a quiet personal setting AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a typical warm entree, including a sauced meal, THEN no BECAUSE both prior and new personal evidence favor skipping extra retract motions to keep the meal efficient.

bite_dipping_preference.user_tendencies: IF food includes a dip or sauce AND dining context is social AND affective state is neutral/unspecified THEN less BECAUSE the user selected reduced dipping for a social meal with sauce, consistent with limiting messiness. IF food is a cold fruit/dessert meal with a topping or dip-like accompaniment AND dining context is social with a person on the left AND affective state is neutral/unspecified THEN less BECAUSE the strawberries-with-whipped-cream social meal shows the user may still want reduced dipping-like application in social settings to avoid messiness. IF food is a cold fruit/dessert meal with a topping or dip-like accompaniment AND dining context involves watching TV on the right AND affective state is neutral/unspecified THEN do not dip BECAUSE the right-TV strawberries meal shows the user did not want dipping-like application in that non-social media context. IF food is a typical warm entree without user-selected dipping behavior AND dining context is social with a person on the right AND affective state is neutral/unspecified THEN do not dip BECAUSE the user selected no dipping for a social warm entree. IF food is a warm meal served with a dip AND dining context involves watching TV in front AND affective state is neutral/unspecified THEN do not dip BECAUSE the user selected no dipping for front-TV meals with available sauces, reinforcing that media-focused meals do not prompt dipping. IF food is a warm meal served with a dip AND dining context is a quiet personal setting AND affective state is neutral/unspecified THEN less BECAUSE the new personal nuggets-and-ketchup meal shows the user prefers some dipping but not heavy dipping in a non-social personal context. IF food is a cold dessert or fruit with topping rather than a dippable sauce AND dining context is not social-pressure-driven AND any affective state THEN do not dip BECAUSE earlier fruit and dessert meals were treated as non-dipping foods and the right-TV fruit meal follows the same pattern. IF food is a typical sauced entree without user-selected dipping behavior AND dining context involves watching TV on the left AND affective state is neutral/unspecified THEN do not dip BECAUSE the user did not request dipping for the meal. IF food is a typical sauced entree without user-selected dipping behavior AND dining context involves watching TV on the right AND affective state is neutral/unspecified THEN do not dip BECAUSE the user declined dipping in both warm and cold right-TV meals. IF food is a typical sauced entree without user-selected dipping behavior AND dining context involves working on a laptop/tablet on the left AND affective state is neutral/unspecified THEN do not dip BECAUSE the user again declined dipping for a sauced entree, reinforcing that sauce present in the dish does not by itself create a dipping preference. IF food is a typical entree served with a dip AND dining context involves working on a laptop/tablet on the right AND affective state is neutral/unspecified THEN do not dip BECAUSE the user selected no dipping for chicken nuggets with ketchup, showing that even clearly dippable foods are not necessarily preferred for dipping in work contexts. IF food has no dippable item or sauce AND any dining context AND any affective state THEN do not dip BECAUSE dipping is not relevant.

wait_before_autocontinue_seconds.user_tendencies: IF dining context is social with a person on the right AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food has no special constraint THEN 100 sec BECAUSE the user selected the middle wait time in a social meal, consistent with allowing time for explicit user control without making automation excessively delayed. IF dining context is social with a person on the left AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a cold fruit/dessert meal THEN 10 sec BECAUSE the left-social meal paired low-effort interaction choices with the shortest timeout, showing that some social meals favor quick automatic progression rather than prolonged waiting. IF dining context involves watching TV in front AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food has no special constraint THEN wait preference is mixed between 10 sec and 1000 sec, with later front-TV evidence selecting the longest delay, so 1000 sec is the better summary when the user also chooses more explicit control in front-TV contexts BECAUSE the user may want substantial time before any automatic progression while focused on media ahead. IF dining context involves watching TV on the right AND affective state is neutral/unspecified AND food is a cold fruit/dessert meal THEN 1000 sec BECAUSE the right-TV strawberries meal shows the user may want a very long timeout in that media context for a light meal.
